content:
11 min read
Just now

--

The opinions expressed in this article about the use of AI coding tools are my own and do not reflect the opinions of my employer. I use coding tools in my daily work, but not to this extreme. This is intended to tell a story of my personal experiences with pushing the limits of what can be done with these tools today.
I set out to see if I could win a Kaggle competition with just $100 worth of AI coding tools and without ever looking at the code produced. While my final submission did pretty well, the journey turned out to be much more frustrating than I anticipated. The TL;DR is: You probably could win one with this approach if you already know the best solution and exactly how to implement it (my solution ranked just outside the top 100 submissions which put me in about the top 3% of all submissions). However, as was my experience, if you need to experiment or iterate on your solution, taking this approach is going to end in frustration.

Introduction
I have been competing about once a year for the last four years in RL-focused Kaggle competitions. Specifically, I have been competing in the Lux AI Challenges. These simulation competitions have become my annual excuse to take whatever new technique or tool I have been itching to try and push it beyond a toy example or a paper I read. In past years that meant things like new architectures, training tricks, or scaling. This year, the obvious candidate was AI coding agents.
So when Kaggle launched Orbit Wars, I decided to run an experiment on myself with three rules:

• I cannot look at the code directly except through conversation with the AI coding agent.

• I cannot modify the code outside of prompting for the AI coding agent to make the changes.

• I can spend 1–2 hours iterating on a plan for the agent to execute while I sleep.
The $100 budget was less a hard constraint and more a forcing function, although it does conveniently align with a Claude Max subscription, which is what I used with Claude Code. I wanted to know what a motivated person with a subscription and no willingness (or ability) to read the code could actually accomplish. The third rule came from necessity rather than principle; I have a day job, so most of my competition time was an hour or two in the evening writing up a plan, kicking off the agent, quickly checking the results the next morning, and making a submission if there had been enough progress to warrant one. I also jumped into the competition a month after it started to further limit the amount of time I could spend on this experiment.
The Game
Orbit Wars is a real-time strategy game played in a continuous 2D space with a sun at the center. Planets orbit the sun, comets fly through on elliptical trajectories, and you win by capturing planets with fleets of ships that travel in straight lines. Your agent is just a function: it receives an observation and returns a list of moves, where each move is a planet to launch from, an angle, and a number of ships. The GIF at the top of this story shows a game being played.
Two details end up mattering a lot for this story. First, the competition had two game formats: 1v1 and 4-player free-for-all, and your single submission had to handle both. Second, evaluation worked like a ladder. Each submission starts with an Elo of 600 and plays episodes against other bots with a similar skill rating, and your rating drifts up or down over hours and days until it stabilizes. You get a maximum of 5 submissions per day. Both of these details come back later in the story.
Early Success
The first couple of weeks felt like I was competing on easy mode.
My evening planning sessions produced a steady stream of infrastructure that would have taken me weeks to write myself: a local runner for self-play games, tooling to download and process episodes from the Kaggle API, and even a small visualization app that rendered which planets a fleet could actually reach given the orbital mechanics (this “reachability mask” ultimately became the attention mask in my Set Transformer architecture). The visualization app was my favorite thing produced during this competition. It is the type of thing I would have never attempted to make in past competitions due to the time it would have taken since web development is outside my domain of expertise. I ported the visualization application over to CodePen (linked below) so you can see how it works. However, during the competition, I never saw a line of code for any of these things. I would describe what I wanted, go to bed, and wake up to a working tool and a summary of what was built.
Orbit Wars 2026 reachability visualization application.The modeling side went just as smoothly. The public episode data from games already played on the ladder is a goldmine for imitation learning, and the agent stood up a behavior cloning pipeline against it without issue other than hitting some rate limits that were easy enough to fix. I set up a daily pipeline to do the following: download episodes from strong players, train a policy to imitate their moves, and package it into a submission. Before long I had a submission sitting comfortably in the top 50.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The submissions that made me believe I had found a winning recipe.Most of that early effort went into perfecting the model architecture, which I ultimately kept throughout the rest of the competition. It is most similar to a Set Transformer with attention masking based on “reachability” of planets to other planets. The prediction heads consisted of a “fire” output, a target planet output, and a fleet fraction output. It took a number of iterations to get this working well and also with low enough latency to meet the competition per-turn timing requirements. You can see this in the slow rise in Elo across my submissions.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Early Elo progress came from perfecting the model architecture.I don’t believe this early success was luck. Looking back, I think it worked for one specific reason: there was a single source of validation, and it was not me. Normally when you can’t read the code, you have no idea whether the agent actually did what you asked or just produced something that looks plausible. Here, I didn’t need to know. The leaderboard doesn’t care how the code looks. If the submission climbed the Elo ladder, the code worked. If it didn’t, it didn’t. Every claim the agent made about its own work was ultimately checked by an external judge that could not be sweet-talked, coerced, or tricked.
For a while, that felt like the whole game had been solved. Then I tried to get clever in an attempt to crack the top 20 of the leaderboard.
The Pivot
With about two weeks left in the competition, I was in the top 50 and thought I had a recipe that would land me right outside of the top 10; all I had to do was scale it. My top 50 submission was just a vanilla behavior cloned policy with persona conditioning (a learned embedding per player, so the model knows whose moves it is imitating) so that I could train on more than just a single submission’s episodes. I had two ideas I was excited about that I thought would boost performance when added to my current solution. The first was training unique agents per game type, one policy specialized for 1v1 and another for the 4-player free-for-all, instead of a single policy trying to do both. The second was reward conditioning: instead of blindly cloning every move in the dataset, condition the policy on the outcome of the episode so that at inference time you can ask it to play like a winner.
Neither idea is exotic, but implementing them is exactly where the whole experiment started to come apart.
The failure mode was almost never that the agent couldn’t write the feature. It was how it wrote the feature. Every new idea arrived wrapped in a new feature flag or a new config parameter, and those additions were rarely backwards compatible with what already existed. Old training configs would silently stop meaning what they used to mean. Worse, each new feature baked in strong assumptions that held in isolation but were violated the moment I tried to mix ideas. The reward conditioning code assumed one policy trained on one merged dataset. The per-game-type code assumed the datasets were split. Ask for both at once and you’d get something that ran, trained, produced a submission, and quietly did the wrong thing the entire time. It wasn’t until I submitted the solution that I realized a couple of days’ worth of training time had been wasted because of some implementation bug.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The period of struggle from my peak submission to the end of the competition, marked by a lot of submissions plagued by bugs.Remember rule number one: I couldn’t just open the code and look. Every bug had to be diagnosed through conversation. The agent would assure me the flags were composed correctly. I would ask pointed questions. It would find the bug, apologize, fix it, and introduce a new flag in the process. My commit count went up and to the right for the entire competition; at one point I even exhausted all my free GitHub runner hours and my CI/CD tests stopped running. While my GitHub commit history looked great, my leaderboard position did not.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Increased commits over the course of the competition.The part that stings in hindsight is that this was a compounding process. Every abandoned experiment left behind its flags, its config parameters, and its assumptions. The codebase I couldn’t see was getting worse in ways I could only infer from the symptoms: the agent’s mistakes became more frequent, and each debugging session took longer than the last. The tool that had built the whole system in the first weeks was now regularly getting lost inside it. I was paying for the difference between writing code and maintaining it. More guardrails early on, or more time spent on the planning sessions before turning on auto-mode overnight, may have prevented this. Reviewing the code certainly would have.
The Double-Edged Leaderboard
This is where the thing that made the early success possible turned around and bit me.
The leaderboard was my only trustworthy oracle, and it is a slow one. Five submissions a day, and hours for each submission’s rating to stabilize enough to tell you anything. In practice, that meant I could test roughly one real hypothesis per day. When the hypotheses were “build a behavior cloning pipeline,” one per day is plenty. When the hypotheses became “did the agent actually wire the reward conditioning flag through the FFA training path this time,” one per day is agonizing.
The obvious fix would have been faster local validation: self-play evaluations, unit tests on the data pipeline, sanity checks on the training runs. The agent even built some of these. But local validation is only as trustworthy as the code that implements it, and I couldn’t read the code. I was in the strange position of having plenty of validation signals and exactly one I could believe. So the final stretch of the competition fell into a miserable rhythm: plan an experiment in the evening, burn a day of submissions confirming it was broken, spend the next evening debugging by conversation, and repeat. You can see this in the steep drop-off in Elo performance after my peak at the two-week mark. At one point I was worried that the AI agent had broken the codebase beyond repair. I would be forced to resubmit the best submission I had as my final one and accept that no more meaningful progress was coming.
The Final Submission
After enough of these cycles, I gave up on cleverness and asked a simpler question: what is the least amount of machinery that captures the one idea that seemed to help?
The answer was almost embarrassing. My best final submission was a behavior cloned policy with reward conditioning,
